source_text: I get by pretty well in French.

reference_translations.text: Me la cavo abbastanza bene in francese.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. cavarsela, 1sg.

reference_translations.text: Mi la cavo abbastanza bene in francese.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the reflexive shifts to me before la, not mi.

explanation: 'Me la cavo bene in francese' = 'I get by well in French.' Cavarsela is a pronominal verb: the reflexive piece becomes me before the frozen la, giving me la cavo.

source_text: L'esame era difficile, ma alla fine se l'è cavata.

reference_translations.text: The exam was hard, but in the end she got through it.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; 'he got through it' equally valid.

reference_translations.text: ...but in the end she cut it off.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: over-literal cavare = to extract; the idiom means to manage/cope.

explanation: 'Se l'è cavata' = 'she/he got through it / managed.' Cavarsela takes essere in the past (se l'è cavata) and the participle is frozen feminine because of the la, whatever the subject's gender.

source_text: We made it! It wasn't easy.

reference_translations.text: Ce l'abbiamo fatta! Non è stato facile.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. farcela past; participle frozen feminine fatta.

reference_translations.text: Ce l'abbiamo fatto! Non è stato facile.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the participle of farcela is always feminine (fatta), never fatto.

explanation: 'Ce l'abbiamo fatta!' = 'We made it!' In the past of farcela the participle is invariably feminine (fatta), agreeing with the frozen la and not with the subject.

source_text: It's late, I'm off.

reference_translations.text: È tardi, me ne vado.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. andarsene, 1sg.

reference_translations.text: È tardi, mi ne vado.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: reflexive shifts to me before ne.

explanation: 'Me ne vado' = 'I'm off / I'm leaving.' Andarsene combines the reflexive with ne, and mi becomes me before ne.

source_text: Se ne sono andati senza nemmeno salutare.

reference_translations.text: They left without even saying goodbye.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; andarsene past with essere and subject agreement.

explanation: 'Se ne sono andati' = 'They left / took off.' Andarsene forms its past with essere, and here the participle agrees with the subject (andati), unlike the frozen-feminine -sela verbs.

source_text: I don't care what people say about me.

reference_translations.text: Me ne frego di quello che dice la gente.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, casual register. fregarsene.

reference_translations.text: Non m'importa quello che dice la gente.
reference_translations.notes: Milder standard alternative without the idiom.

explanation: 'Me ne frego di...' = 'I don't give a damn about...' Fregarsene is a casual pronominal verb built on ne; it governs its complement with di.

source_text: It takes two hours to get to Milan by train.

reference_translations.text: Ci vogliono due ore per arrivare a Milano in treno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Impersonal volerci, plural agreement.

reference_translations.text: Ci vuole due ore per arrivare a Milano in treno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: volerci agrees with the requirement; due ore is plural, so ci vogliono.

explanation: 'Ci vogliono due ore' = 'It takes two hours.' No personal subject, so impersonal volerci; and it agrees with due ore, hence the plural ci vogliono.

source_text: I take half an hour to get ready in the morning.

reference_translations.text: Ci metto mezz'ora a prepararmi la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Personal subject, so metterci.

reference_translations.text: Ci vuole mezz'ora a prepararmi la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: with a personal subject (io) Italian uses metterci, not the impersonal volerci.

explanation: 'Ci metto mezz'ora' = 'I take half an hour.' A person spends the time, so metterci with a personal subject: io ci metto.

source_text: Il colore delle pareti non c'entra niente con l'arredamento.

reference_translations.text: The colour of the walls has nothing to do with the furniture.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; entrarci = to be relevant.

explanation: 'Non c'entra niente' = 'It has nothing to do with it.' Entrarci means to be connected or relevant, not to enter physically.

source_text: It took three hours, but in the end we finished everything.

reference_translations.text: Ci sono volute tre ore, ma alla fine abbiamo finito tutto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. volerci past with essere, agreement with tre ore.

reference_translations.text: Ci ha voluto tre ore, ma alla fine abbiamo finito tutto.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: volerci takes essere, not avere: ci sono volute.

explanation: 'Ci sono volute tre ore' = 'It took three hours.' In the past volerci takes essere and agrees with the requirement, so with tre ore (feminine plural) it is ci sono volute.

source_text: I really care about this job.

reference_translations.text: Ci tengo molto a questo lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. tenerci a, 1sg.

reference_translations.text: Tengo molto questo lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: without ci and the preposition a, the 'care about' sense is lost.

explanation: 'Ci tengo molto a questo lavoro' = 'I really care about this job.' Tenerci a qualcosa is the pronominal verb for caring about something; the ci is lexicalised and the complement takes a.

source_text: Non ce la faccio più a lavorare in queste condizioni.

reference_translations.text: I can't cope any more with working in these conditions.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; farcela = to cope/manage.

reference_translations.text: I don't make it any more working in these conditions.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: literal fare reading; the idiom means to cope, not to make.

explanation: 'Non ce la faccio più' = 'I can't cope any more.' Farcela means to manage or cope; ce la is frozen, so a literal 'making' reading misfires.

source_text: Non prendertela, non volevo offenderti.

reference_translations.text: Don't take it to heart, I didn't mean to offend you.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; prendersela = to get upset.

reference_translations.text: Don't take it, I didn't want to offend you.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: misses the idiom; prendersela is to take offence, not to take an object.

explanation: 'Non prendertela' = 'Don't take it to heart.' Prendersela means to get offended or upset; the la is frozen and there is no literal object being taken.

source_text: Ce l'ha con me da settimane e non so perché.

reference_translations.text: He's had it in for me for weeks and I don't know why.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; avercela con = to be angry with.

reference_translations.text: He has it for me for weeks and I don't know why.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: literal 'have' reading; avercela con means to bear a grudge.

explanation: 'Ce l'ha con me' = 'He's got it in for me.' Avercela con qualcuno means to be annoyed at someone; despite the shape of avere, nothing is being possessed.

source_text: Non me la sento di dirglielo stasera.

reference_translations.text: I don't feel up to telling him tonight.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; sentirsela di = to feel up to.

explanation: 'Non me la sento di dirglielo' = 'I don't feel up to telling him.' Sentirsela di fare qualcosa is the idiom for feeling able or willing to do something.

source_text: Do you have a lighter? Yes, I've got one.

reference_translations.text: Hai un accendino? Sì, ce l'ho.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. The have-it answer needs ce l'ho.

reference_translations.text: Hai un accendino? Sì, l'ho.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: bare l'ho sounds unfinished; the natural answer is ce l'ho.

explanation: 'Sì, ce l'ho' = 'Yes, I've got one.' To say you have something, spoken Italian uses ce l'ho, not the bare l'ho.

source_text: Do you have the keys? Yes, I've got them.

reference_translations.text: Hai le chiavi? Sì, ce le ho.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Plural feminine object, so ce le ho with no elision.

reference_translations.text: Hai le chiavi? Sì, ce l'ho.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: le chiavi is plural, so ce le ho; ce l'ho is the singular.

explanation: 'Sì, ce le ho' = 'Yes, I've got them.' With a plural object (le chiavi) the pronoun is le and does not elide, so it stays ce le ho, not the singular ce l'ho.

source_text: In the end they managed and went home happy.

reference_translations.text: Alla fine ce l'hanno fatta e se ne sono andati a casa contenti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; farcela + andarsene together.

reference_translations.text: Alla fine ce l'hanno fatto e se ne sono andati a casa contenti.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: farcela's participle is feminine (fatta).

explanation: 'Ce l'hanno fatta e se ne sono andati' = 'They managed and went off.' Two pronominal verbs side by side: farcela freezes its participle feminine (fatta), while andarsene agrees with the subject (andati).

source_text: Con i bambini ci vuole molta pazienza.

reference_translations.text: You need a lot of patience with children.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; impersonal volerci rendered with 'you need'.

reference_translations.text: It takes a lot of patience with children.
reference_translations.notes: Also fine; impersonal 'it takes' reading.

explanation: 'Ci vuole molta pazienza' = 'You need a lot of patience.' Impersonal volerci is often best rendered in English as 'you need' or 'it takes'.

source_text: How long does it take to learn Italian?

reference_translations.text: Quanto tempo ci vuole per imparare l'italiano?
reference_translations.notes: Standard; general question, so impersonal volerci.

reference_translations.text: Quanto tempo ci metti per imparare l'italiano?
reference_translations.notes: Shifts to 'how long does it take YOU' (metterci); fine only if a personal subject is meant.

explanation: 'Quanto tempo ci vuole?' = 'How long does it take?' A general, impersonal question uses volerci; metterci would make it personal ('how long does it take you').

source_text: As soon as they saw the police, they legged it.

reference_translations.text: Appena hanno visto la polizia, se la sono data a gambe.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; darsela a gambe with essere and frozen feminine data.

reference_translations.text: Appena hanno visto la polizia, se l'hanno data a gambe.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: darsela is reflexive and takes essere, not avere.

explanation: 'Se la sono data a gambe' = 'They legged it.' Darsela a gambe takes essere and freezes its participle feminine (data), and the tail a gambe cannot be varied.

source_text: Ce l'ha messa tutta, ma alla fine non è bastato.

reference_translations.text: He gave it everything he had, but in the end it wasn't enough.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; mettercela tutta = to give it your all.

reference_translations.text: He put it all in there, but in the end it wasn't enough.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: over-literal mettere reading; the idiom is about effort, not placing an object.

explanation: 'Ce l'ha messa tutta' = 'He gave it his all.' Mettercela tutta freezes ce la and requires the tail tutta; read literally as 'putting' it makes no sense, which is the signal that an idiom is in play.

source_text: He never forgets a slight; he really bears a grudge.

reference_translations.text: Non dimentica mai un torto: se l'è proprio legata al dito.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; legarsela al dito.

reference_translations.text: Non dimentica mai un torto: se l'è proprio legata alle dita.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the frozen tail is singular al dito and cannot be pluralised.

explanation: 'Se l'è legata al dito' = 'He bore a grudge.' Legarsela al dito is frozen: the participle is feminine (legata) for the frozen la, and the tail stays singular al dito.
